ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general toast entry (FOODON_03301710) but no specific entry for thick-cut toast with peanut butter spread. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for peanut butter thick toast. A000E (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 226529007 refers to toast as a substance category; no specific code exists for peanut butter thick toast. Closest parent used.

serving.description: 2 slices thick toast with peanut butter
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 2 slices of thick-cut white toast (about 120 g total) spread with peanut butter, as typically served at a Singapore kopitiam.

nutrient_source.title: Peanut butter toast / bread with peanut butter — composite estimate from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Bread, white, commercially prepared; Peanut butter, smooth style)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo does not list a dedicated 'peanut butter thick toast' entry. Nutrients estimated from a weighted composite: approximately 65 g thick white bread (HPB Foodo: white bread, per 100 g) and 35 g peanut butter (USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 16098, smooth peanut butter per 100 g), scaled to 100 g combined. This reflects the typical Toast Box-style serving ratio. Values are rounded to one decimal place.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White bread GI reference; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore-style peanut butter thick toast. GI of 70 is assigned based on white bread GI reference value (GI=70, glucose=100 scale) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables. Peanut butter addition lowers the effective glycemic response slightly; however, the dominant carbohydrate vehicle is white bread, and GI=70 is retained as a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 53.4) / 100 ≈ 37.4, where carbohydrate per 120 g serving = 44.5 × 1.2 = 53.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~372 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit peanut butter thick toast to occasional consumption, as one serving contributes approximately 372 mg sodium (~16% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), and should choose reduced-sodium peanut butter where available.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 70) and high glycemic load per serving (~37.4)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume peanut butter thick toast sparingly, as the high GI white bread base produces a rapid postprandial glucose rise; pairing with a protein source or choosing wholegrain bread may attenuate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.9 g per 100 g) and sodium (~310 mg per 100 g); peanut butter provides beneficial monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, peanut butter thick toast is acceptable in moderation; the unsaturated fat profile of peanut butter is cardioprotective, but the sodium content and refined white bread base warrant portion control and preference for wholegrain alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peanut Butter Thick Toast is a popular Singaporean kopitiam (traditional coffee shop) breakfast item consisting of thick-cut white bread that is toasted until golden and crisp, then generously spread with smooth or crunchy peanut butter. It is a signature offering at chains such as Toast Box and Ya Kun Kaya Toast, and is commonly enjoyed alongside a cup of kopi (local coffee) or teh (local tea) and soft-boiled eggs. The bread is characteristically thicker than standard sandwich bread, yielding a satisfying crunch on the outside with a soft, pillowy interior. The peanut butter spread adds a rich, nutty flavour and creamy texture. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 348 kcal, 10.2 g protein, 14.8 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from peanut butter), and 44.5 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is high (~70) due to the refined white bread base, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate at ~310 mg per 100 g. The dish is vegetarian and halal-friendly but contains gluten and peanut allergens. It is a culturally significant comfort food in Singapore, representing the Hainanese kopitiam tradition brought by early Chinese immigrants.
common_user_queries: What is peanut butter thick toast?
How many calories in peanut toast from Toast Box?

What is the GI of peanut butter toast?

How much sodium in kopitiam peanut toast?
What is the difference between kaya toast and peanut toast?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry exists for peanut butter thick toast as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of HPB Foodo white bread data and USDA SR Legacy peanut butter data, reflecting a typical Toast Box-style serving ratio. GI is estimated from the white bread reference value in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the adapted composite methodology rather than a direct measurement of the finished dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates based on approximately 65% thick white bread and 35% peanut butter by weight per 100 g of the combined food, derived from HPB Foodo (white bread) and USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 16098 (smooth peanut butter). Actual nutrient content will vary by brand, bread thickness, and peanut butter quantity applied. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not consistently reported across the composite source components at sufficient precision to assign a reliable value. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in either HPB Foodo white bread or USDA SR Legacy peanut butter entries. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo white bread source. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: peanut butter contains no cholesterol (plant-derived), and white bread contains negligible cholesterol confirmed at 0 mg per USDA SR Legacy. 6. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: neither white bread nor peanut butter contains meaningful vitamin C per USDA SR Legacy. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: both white bread and peanut butter are plant-derived or non-animal products with no B12 per USDA SR Legacy. 8. GI of 70 is a conservative analogous estimate based on white bread reference; actual GI of the composite dish with peanut butter may be slightly lower due to fat and protein moderating glycemic response. 9. Halal status assumes standard commercial peanut butter without animal-derived additives; consumers should verify product-specific halal certification. 10. The food_name has been normalised from 'Peanut Thick Toast' (field survey menu name) to 'Peanut Butter Thick Toast' as the canonical English name; the original menu name is retained in aliases.

improvement_actions_taken: Normalised food_name from field survey menu label 'Peanut Thick Toast' to canonical 'Peanut Butter Thick Toast'; original retained in aliases.
Constructed composite nutrient profile from HPB Foodo (white bread) and USDA SR Legacy (peanut butter) at 65:35 weight ratio.
Assigned GI=70 from University of Sydney GI Database white bread reference (Atkinson et al. 2021) as analogous estimate; documented basis in gi_source.
Added all mandatory Singaporean multilingual names: Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_d_mcg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-absence justifications in notes.
Documented confirmed-zero values (cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg) with 'confirmed zero' justification phrases in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent — all 17 alias strings cross-checked.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: 'closest_parent' for all three ontology systems.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
